rationale: The citing paper uses Keller, Lipkus, and Rimer (2003) repeatedly in its conceptual development, not just as a background citation. It relies on that paper's findings about mood valence and health-message framing to motivate its core premise that emotions affect processing of aversive health information via coping resources and mood-repair motives. The authors then position their own contribution as moving beyond valence/framing by introducing discrete-emotion self/other relatedness and referent compatibility effects.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Keller et al. (2003) as prior evidence that mood valence shapes the persuasiveness of health messages, especially under gain/loss framing. It extends that line by testing additional appraisal dimensions of discrete emotions and showing how emotion-message referent compatibility changes processing and persuasion.
